---
title: "Dataset Preparation"
author: "MAA, Data Science Lab, Progress and Health Public Foundation (FPS), Regional Ministry of Health, Andalucía, Sevilla"
---

Template author: MAA, Data Science Lab, Progress and Health Public Foundation (FPS), Regional Ministry of Health, Andalucía, Sevilla.

## Data Scientist Style Recommendations

Use this notebook as the single source of truth for dataset construction. Keep extraction, minimal harmonization, cohort definition, feature engineering, missingness handling, imputation, validation, and export in clearly separated sections. Read SQL from files when SQL is part of the source layer, preserve long/event-level domain tables before creating wide analytical features, document every aggregation rule, and render an imputation report immediately after imputation. All code, comments, labels, and narrative must be written in English.

## Setup

This section initializes the execution environment and defines project-level constants, paths, and reproducibility settings.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- numpy
- pathlib
- warnings
- datetime
- tqdm (optional, for progress monitoring)

Recommended tasks:
- define input and output directories
- configure warnings
- set fixed parameters and study cutoffs
- define reusable project constants
- define the unit of analysis early, such as patient, admission, user, transaction, or episode
- define the expected final dataset object name, usually `final_dataset`
- create output directories before any write operation

All code, comments, printed labels, and narrative text in this notebook must be written in English.

## Optional SQL Input Layer

This section is used only when one or more source domains are extracted from SQL files at the start of the dataset pipeline.

Typical libraries:
- pathlib
- pandas
- sqlalchemy, duckdb, or a project-specific database connector

Recommended methodology:
- store SQL files in `analysis/sql/`
- read SQL from files rather than embedding long SQL strings in the notebook
- keep SQL focused on extraction, light source cleaning, and reproducible filters
- keep cohort definitions, outcomes, feature engineering, and imputation in this notebook
- load each SQL result into a clearly named source-domain object
- record row counts, unique identifiers, date ranges, and extraction timestamps when available

Typical pattern:

```python
sql_path = Path("sql/demographics.sql")
query = sql_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
demographics_raw = pd.read_sql_query(query, connection)
```

This section may be skipped when all inputs are files such as CSV, parquet, or Excel.

## Pipeline Functions

This section defines the reusable functions that support the full dataset pipeline.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- numpy
- scikit-learn
- pathlib

Typical functions:
- source loaders for text, CSV, parquet, or Excel inputs
- column standardization helpers
- date parsing helpers
- text normalization helpers
- entity identifier cleaning helpers
- source cache and manifest helpers
- quality control helpers
- imputation helpers

Recommended methodology:
- define all reusable functions here
- avoid redefining functions later in the notebook
- keep functions modular and side-effect free
- prefer structured parsers and stable helper functions over ad hoc string manipulation
- make cache invalidation explicit when caching raw or harmonized sources

## Data Sources and Input Management

This section documents all input sources used in the project and how they are accessed.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- pathlib

Possible input sources:
- SQL files in `analysis/sql/`
- delimited text files
- CSV files
- parquet files
- Excel files
- precomputed intermediate files

Recommended methodology:
- make the source of each dataset explicit
- load one source domain into one object
- keep loading logic separate from downstream transformations
- maintain a source inventory with domain, path or SQL file, expected grain, key columns, and date fields
- prefer cached parquet copies of large raw sources when repeated parsing is slow
- write a small manifest for cached sources including source path, size, modification time, row count, and creation time

## Data Extraction and Import

This section performs the actual import of all source datasets required for the analysis.

Typical libraries:
- pandas

Typical methodology:
- execute SQL source files from the optional SQL layer when applicable
- use `pd.read_csv()` for text or CSV files
- use `pd.read_parquet()` for parquet files
- use `pd.read_excel()` for Excel files
- apply only minimal harmonization at import time, such as:
  - column name normalization
  - identifier cleaning
  - date parsing
  - type coercion
  - standardization of obvious malformed values

This section should not include:
- cohort filtering
- feature engineering
- imputation
- analytical derivations

## Initial Source QC

This section performs a first structural review of each imported dataset before integration.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- numpy

Typical checks:
- number of rows and columns
- unique identifiers
- duplicated keys
- missingness in key variables
- date parsing success
- obvious invalid values

Recommended methodology:
- validate each source independently
- detect ingestion problems before merging datasets

## Dataset Construction

This section integrates all imported sources into a single analytical structure.

Typical libraries:
- pandas

Typical methodology:
- use `merge`, `concat`, `groupby`, `pivot`, and reshaping operations
- define the unit of analysis explicitly
- validate row counts and identifiers after each merge
- keep the construction sequence transparent and reproducible
- keep event-level or longitudinal domain tables before producing wide analytical features
- document every aggregation rule used to move from long to wide format

Typical checks after each merge:
- row count changes
- duplicated identifiers
- unexpected inflation or loss of records

Recommended long-to-wide rules:
- `first`: earliest valid event date or first observed state
- `latest`: most recent valid baseline value
- `ever`: binary indicator for any qualifying event
- `count`: number of events, measurements, records, or distinct categories
- `sum`: cumulative burden or exposure
- `min` / `max`: first and last observed dates

When possible, retain both:
- a long table for future time-to-event, trajectory, or sensitivity analyses
- a wide table for the primary analytical dataset

## Cohort Definition

This section applies the inclusion and exclusion criteria that define the analytical population.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- numpy

Recommended methodology:
- apply filters sequentially
- store counts after each filtering step
- keep cohort logic separate from feature engineering
- ensure that every exclusion is reproducible and traceable
- store cohort flow counts in a structured object such as `cohort_counts`
- export cohort counts as JSON for downstream CONSORT or flow diagrams

Typical criteria:
- age restrictions
- therapy eligibility
- minimum follow-up
- valid baseline window
- data completeness requirements

## Feature Engineering

This section derives the analytical variables used in downstream analysis.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- numpy

Typical operations:
- variable renaming
- categorical recoding
- date-derived features
- baseline and follow-up summaries
- exposure derivation
- outcome derivation
- subgroup indicators
- disease classification variables

Recommended methodology:
- create features only after the cohort is fixed
- document derived variables clearly
- keep domain logic explicit
- avoid deriving the same variable again in downstream notebooks
- apply domain prefixes only at final export unless an established project convention requires otherwise

Recommended naming conventions:
- `{domain}_` prefixes for final exported variables, such as `lab_`, `com_`, `rx_`, or `adm_`
- `_date` for dates tied to events or measurements
- `_missing` for missingness indicators created before imputation
- `_flag` for derived binary quality, eligibility, or rule indicators
- `_n` or `n_` for counts

## Missing Data Assessment

This section evaluates the extent and structure of missingness in the analytical variables.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- numpy

Recommended methodology:
- quantify missingness per variable
- review missingness in key baseline features
- check whether missingness differs by subgroup, treatment, or cohort
- identify variables eligible for imputation and variables that should remain untouched
- distinguish true missingness from structural absence
- treat absence of an event record as absence only when the source data model supports that interpretation
- define imputation eligibility before running any imputation

Typical outputs:
- missing counts
- missing percentages
- optional subgroup-specific missingness summaries
- list of variables excluded from imputation and the reason

## Imputation Strategy and Application

This section defines and applies the missing data handling rules in a consistent way across variable types.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- numpy
- scikit-learn

Recommended baseline strategy:
- continuous variables:
  - simple median imputation when a deterministic and transparent rule is sufficient
- categorical variables:
  - explicit missing category such as `"Missing"` when clinically and analytically appropriate
- binary variables:
  - preserve as missing unless there is a justified and documented coding rule
- optional missingness indicators:
  - create flags such as `var_missing = var.isna().astype(int)`

Advanced strategy when multivariable structure matters:
- use `sklearn.impute.IterativeImputer`
- use a stable estimator such as `BayesianRidge`
- impute eligible baseline variables jointly
- include auxiliary predictors when clinically justified
- constrain impossible values when needed

Recommended rules:
- apply the same imputation logic by data type across the project
- do not impute outcomes
- do not impute cohort-defining variables unless explicitly justified
- do not impute event dates whose missingness encodes absence of the event
- create missingness indicators before imputation
- use clear missingness thresholds, such as no action for 0%, impute for eligible variables up to a defined threshold, and skip variables above that threshold
- document which variables are eligible for imputation and which are excluded
- cache imputed outputs only when the parent dataset fingerprint and imputation rules are also stored

## Imputation Report

This section must be printed or rendered immediately after the imputation step.

Typical contents:
- variables imputed
- variables not imputed
- missing percentage before imputation
- method applied per variable
- missingness indicators created
- variables skipped because they are outcomes, cohort-defining variables, dates, identifiers, constants, or above the missingness threshold
- any imputation cache hit or cache write

Recommended outputs:
- compact table sorted by missing percentage
- counts by action, such as imputed, median-filled, categorical-missing-level, skipped, and untouched
- explicit statement that outcomes and cohort-defining variables were not imputed

## Post-Imputation Validation

This section verifies that imputation behaved as expected after the imputation report has been generated.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- numpy

Typical checks:
- no remaining unexpected missingness in imputed variables
- no impossible generated values
- no negative values in variables that must be non-negative
- preserved shape and alignment of the dataset
- comparison of distributions before and after imputation

Recommended methodology:
- validate imputation outputs before finalizing the dataset
- confirm that only intended variables were modified

## Data Validation

This section performs structural and clinical validation after construction, feature engineering, and imputation.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- numpy

Typical checks:
- duplicated rows
- duplicated identifiers
- join integrity
- temporal consistency
- clinically implausible values
- out-of-range values
- consistency between related variables

Recommended methodology:
- run both structural and domain-aware QC
- keep validation explicit and reproducible
- fail early or print explicit warnings when key assumptions are violated
- drop or flag constant variables before statistical testing or modeling

## CONSORT Diagrams

This section documents the cohort selection workflow in a reproducible way.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- plotnine, matplotlib, or other plotting tools if needed

Recommended methodology:
- ensure that diagram counts match the filtering logic exactly
- distinguish the main analytical cohort from broader or alternative cohorts

### Primary cohort

This subsection presents the main analytical cohort used for the primary analysis.

### Secondary or full cohort

This subsection presents a broader or alternative cohort used for sensitivity analyses or descriptive extension.

## Final Dataset QC

This section performs the final review before saving the analytical dataset.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- numpy

Typical checks:
- final dimensions
- final variable availability
- missingness after imputation
- balance of outcomes or exposures
- sanity checks on derived variables
- consistency of final identifiers

Recommended methodology:
- use this section as the final release gate before export

## Preview

This section provides a compact inspection of the resulting dataset.

Typical tools:
- `head()`
- `sample()`
- `info()`
- selected summaries

Recommended methodology:
- inspect only
- do not introduce new transformations here

## Save Final Dataset

This section saves the final analytical datasets and any required intermediate outputs.

Typical libraries:
- pandas
- pathlib

Typical outputs:
- parquet
- CSV
- optional metadata summaries
- cohort counts JSON
- column dictionary or column manifest
- retained longitudinal tables, when applicable

Recommended methodology:
- use explicit object names such as `primary_dataset`, `full_dataset`, or `final_dataset`
- save to stable output paths
- ensure that exported files correspond exactly to the validated final objects
- treat `final_dataset` as the single source of truth for downstream notebooks
- downstream notebooks should load exported artifacts rather than re-deriving variables

## Session Info

This section records the execution context for reproducibility.

Typical libraries:
- sys
- platform
- pandas
- numpy
- sklearn

Recommended outputs:
- Python version
- package versions
- execution date
